user_query:
I need to decide whom to commission for our comprehensive study on interpreting ancient myths. Please work entirely from the local files in input/brief/editorial_brief.md and the proposals under input/proposals/.

Inputs to use:
- input/brief/editorial_brief.md
- input/proposals/proposal_green.md
- input/proposals/proposal_saffron.md

Deliverables:
1) Rewrite each proposal into a standardized executive summary (180–220 words each) that captures, in your own edited prose, the proposal’s Approach, Corpus, Methods, Deliverables, and Timeline, plus include exactly three strengths and one risk as bullet points drawn from the proposal content.
   - Save as:
     - output/edited_summaries/proposal_green.summary.md
     - output/edited_summaries/proposal_saffron.summary.md
   - Each summary should begin with two lines: Title: <proposal title> and Scholar: <scholar name>, then the narrative summary paragraph, then the bullet list of strengths and one risk. Do not invent facts not present in the proposals; paraphrase is fine but keep meaning faithful.

2) Create a comparison and recommendation memo that aligns with the editorial brief’s hard constraints and priorities.
   - Save as: output/comparison_report.md
   - Include:
     - A one-paragraph synthesis contrasting scope and methods.
     - A compact comparison section (bullets or a simple two-column layout) covering for each proposal: Timeline (months), Corpus breadth (as stated), Methods (as stated), and Deliverables (as stated).
     - An explicit sentence identifying which proposal satisfies the 10‑month manuscript deadline specified in the brief, quoting the timeline months from the proposals.
     - A clear recommendation with a 2–4 sentence justification tied to at least two priorities from input/brief/editorial_brief.md.
     - A line listing the basenames of all .md proposal files discovered under input/proposals (e.g., proposal_green.md, proposal_saffron.md) to confirm directory inspection.

3) Draft two email messages:
   - Offer email to the scholar whose proposal meets the brief’s hard 10‑month deadline. Include:
     - First line: Subject: Commission Offer: <proposal title>
     - Greeting to the correct scholar by name, a concise offer referencing the project title, the 10‑month manuscript due date, and the deliverables as described in their proposal. Keep under 200 words.
     - Save as: output/emails/offer_<scholar_lastname>.txt (use lowercase lastname from the proposal file you select).
   - Decline email to the other scholar. Include:
     - First line: Subject: Regarding Your Proposal: <proposal title>
     - A polite and appreciative note that mentions one specific positive aspect of their proposal and the schedule reason for declining. Keep under 200 words.
     - Save as: output/emails/decline_<scholar_lastname>.txt (use lowercase lastname).

4) Produce a small metadata file to support verification:
   - Save as: output/metadata/processed_proposals.json
   - JSON array of objects with fields: file (string, basename), title (string), scholar_last (string), timeline_months (number). Populate values by extracting from each proposal.

Additional constraints:
- Inspect input/proposals/ to discover and confirm the .md proposal files; ignore non‑.md files if any appear.
- Base all assertions on the local inputs; do not add outside facts.
- Keep naming and subject lines exactly as specified.
- Do not modify the original input files; produce new files under output/ as specified.

If anything is unclear in the inputs, note it briefly at the end of output/comparison_report.md under a heading “Open Questions.” All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
